title: Parental practices and educational achievement: social class, race, andhabitus
abstract: Abstract A large, nationally representative database of American elementary school students was used to quantitatively assess the complex ways in which race intersects with social class, affecting parenting strategies that in turn produce various educational outcomes among children. The determinants and consequences of parental practices associated with middle‐class families – what Lareau terms 'concerted cultivation' – among White and African American students were examined. The findings reveal that cultural differences in child‐rearing occur along class, race, and gender boundaries. Keywords: parental practiceseducational achievement Notes 1. Although there are various ways to conceptualize parental social class, for the purpose of this study I use family SES as a proxy for social class. 2. Lareau made a considerable effort to show that 'accomplishment of natural growth' is a distinct parenting strategy utilized by working and poor families and is not just a lack of 'concerted cultivation'. However, data limitations do not allow for separate tests of the 'accomplishment of natural growth' effects. 3. In calculations not shown, I used three separate SES measures for all models. All three SES components (family income, occupational prestige, and education) showed significant effects on the outcomes, with education having the largest effect.
journal: British Journal of Sociology of Education
